skill.name: react-patterns
skill.description: Guidance for applying modern React patterns, including hooks, composition, state selection, performance, testing, and TypeScript practices.
skill.summary: Use concise React guidance to choose component, hook, state, performance, testing, and TypeScript patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported browser storage and weak cryptography patterns, but review found only Markdown guidance and no executable code. The browser storage hit is a useLocalStorage hook example, and the weak cryptography hits are substring matches inside normal React text such as description, Design, and provides. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, command execution, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Browser Storage Hook Example
low_findings.description: The static finding at SKILL.md line 39 references useLocalStorage as an example of when to extract a custom hook. The file contains documentation only and does not access browser storage or handle stored secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is a Markdown table entry, not executable JavaScript. No evidence found of localStorage calls, secret handling, or browser storage access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The static findings at SKILL.md lines 3, 13, 24, and 95 appear to match text fragments in words like description, Design, and provides. The skill contains no cryptographic APIs, algorithms, hashes, ciphers, or security-sensitive code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter text, section headings, or component design guidance. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES usage, encryption routines, or related code.

content.value_statement: React projects often suffer from unclear component structure, overused effects, and premature optimization. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concise guidance for choosing practical React patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use server, client, presentational, and container components.
Guides custom hook extraction for storage, debounce, fetch, and form patterns.
Compares state management choices for local, shared, server, and global state.
Summarizes React 19 hooks, compiler expectations, and composition patterns.
Provides testing, error handling, performance, and TypeScript pattern guidance.
limitations: It is a guidance document and does not include runnable code generators.
It does not inspect a project automatically without the assistant reading files separately.
It gives general React advice and may need adaptation for a specific framework.
It does not replace profiling, accessibility testing, or production review.

use_cases.title: Plan Component Structure
use_cases.description: Choose component boundaries, data flow, and composition patterns before building a feature.

use_cases.title: Review React Refactors
use_cases.description: Check whether hooks, state placement, error boundaries, and performance changes follow practical React guidance.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Practices
use_cases.description: Create a shared baseline for React 19, TypeScript props, testing priorities, and common anti-patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Component Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-patterns skill to recommend component types and state placement for this feature. Explain the choice in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: Extract the Right Hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-patterns skill to review this repeated React logic. Tell me whether it should become a custom hook and why.

prompt_templates.title: Review Performance Choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-patterns skill to evaluate this component for premature optimization, memoization needs, and list rendering issues.

prompt_templates.title: Audit React Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-patterns skill to audit this React feature for component design, state management, composition, errors, testing, and TypeScript patterns.

output_examples.input: A form feature needs optimistic updates, validation, and retry behavior.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends form state with a focused hook, useActionState for submission state, an error boundary around the feature, and tests for retry and validation states.

output_examples.input: A large list renders slowly after adding filters and derived totals.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests profiling first, virtualizing the list if rendering is the bottleneck, and memoizing only expensive derived calculations.

output_examples.input: A component passes many props through five nested levels.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies prop drilling and recommends context or compound components when the shared state belongs to a subtree.

best_practices: Start with clear component responsibilities before adding abstractions.
Profile performance problems before adding memoization or virtualization.
Test user-visible behavior, edge cases, error states, and accessibility.
anti_patterns: Do not use effects for work better handled by rendering or server components.
Do not choose global state before checking whether local or subtree state is enough.
Do not use array indexes as keys when stable identifiers are available.

faq.question: Does this skill generate React code?
faq.answer: It provides pattern guidance. An assistant can use it while writing code, but the skill itself is documentation.

faq.question: Does it cover React 19?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for useActionState, useOptimistic, the use hook, and compiler-friendly pure components.

faq.question: Can it help choose state management?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares useState, useReducer, Context, React Query, SWR, Zustand, and Redux Toolkit by complexity and scope.

faq.question: Is it specific to TypeScript?
faq.answer: It includes TypeScript props and common React types, but most design guidance also applies to JavaScript projects.

faq.question: Does it include security-sensitive behavior?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file is Markdown guidance and contains no executable commands, network calls, or secret access.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Frontend developers, reviewers, and engineering leads can use it to make React architecture decisions more consistent.
